summary: Verified iteration 17 switch from hand-rolled blob+anchor download to file-saver (saveAs) end-to-end via Playwright with download interception. All three export buttons (Export Excel + Export CSV on AllocationDetail, Export Debtors on /debtors) now trigger real, on-disk file downloads with correct content. Negative test (token cleared) still surfaces an error toast containing '401' — no silent failure. Original user-reported regression (button confirms but no physical file in Chrome) is resolved at the API/blob level; file-saver is correctly wired.

integration_issues.flow: Export error message readability (carry-over from iteration 16, still present)
integration_issues.issue: In /app/frontend/src/lib/api.js downloadAuthed(), on a non-OK response the error toast shows 'Download failed (401): HTTP 401' instead of the backend's actual detail ('Not authenticated'). The negative-test toast text we observed was literally 'Download failed (401): HTTP 401'. Either await res.json() or await res.text() is throwing and the catch block falls back to the generic `HTTP ${status}` string. The acceptance check (toast must contain '401' or 'Not authenticated') still PASSES because '401' is in the message, but the human-readable backend detail is being lost. Recommended fix: read body as text first, then try JSON.parse on it, e.g. `const raw = await res.text(); try { const j = JSON.parse(raw); detail = j?.detail || j?.message || raw; } catch { detail = raw || \`HTTP ${res.status}\`; }`.

action_items: Optional polish (carry-over): in downloadAuthed (/app/frontend/src/lib/api.js), avoid losing the backend's `detail` on failure. Read body as text once then JSON.parse, so the auth-failure toast reads 'Download failed (401): Not authenticated' instead of 'HTTP 401'.
critical_code_review_comments: file-saver import is correct (`import { saveAs } from 'file-saver';`) and used as `saveAs(blob, filename)` — the canonical safe path; this fixes the silent-failure case the user hit in their Chrome where the hand-rolled URL.createObjectURL + anchor.click() approach was being swallowed.
downloadAuthed still keeps `credentials: 'include'` so cookie-based fallback continues to work alongside the Bearer header.
Pre-existing issue: response body consumed twice on the error path → toast loses the backend `detail` and falls back to `HTTP 401`. Acceptable but worth fixing for UX.
seed_data_creation: Reused existing seeded allocation run XL_export_344182 (id c4ff2b7a-f66a-46ad-bd8a-510a62ca8aa7, status=done).
context_for_next_testing_agent: Verified observations from live Playwright run: (1) XLSX download via [data-testid=export-xlsx] → saved 468,083 bytes, first 2 bytes = 'PK', suggested filename 'allocation-XL_export_344182.xlsx'. (2) CSV download via [data-testid=export-csv] → saved 854,837 bytes, first line 'Bank Date,Bank Reference,Bank Payer,Bank Amount (£),Status,Confidence,Why matche…', suggested filename 'allocation-XL_export_344182.csv'. (3) Debtors export via [data-testid=export-debtors] → saved 86,495 bytes, first line starts with 'Debtor,Flagged,Total Outstanding (£)…', filename 'debtor-report.csv'. (4) Success toasts: 'Excel ready' / 'CSV ready' / no error toast. (5) Negative test: cleared localStorage.ebrr_access_token AND cookies, clicked export-xlsx → error toast appeared with text 'Download failed (401): HTTP 401' — contains '401' so requirement met, though backend's 'Not authenticated' string is being lost. Network: GET /api/allocations/{id}/export-xlsx returned 401. No download event fired in the negative case (good). Playwright pattern that works: `async with page.expect_download(timeout=60000) as info: await page.click(...); dl = await info.value; await dl.save_as(path)`. SignIn selectors are data-testid=signin-email/signin-password/signin-submit. List endpoint /api/allocations returns a plain JSON array.
rca_of_the_issue: User-reported (iteration 17): in normal Chrome the previous hand-rolled download helper (URL.createObjectURL + programmatic <a>.click() + revoke) was being silently swallowed — the success toast fired but no file landed on disk. Iteration 17 swaps the save step to file-saver's `saveAs(blob, filename)` (v2.0.5), which encapsulates the cross-browser quirks (Chrome download throttling, Safari quirks, Firefox blob URL revocation timing, etc.). Playwright run on this iteration confirms all three exports actually emit a download event AND write the correct bytes to disk. The earlier hand-rolled approach worked in headless Chromium but failed in regular Chrome — file-saver works in both.
